# Amazon Carbon Credit Project Lost More Forest Than Unprotected Jungle Nearby, Satellite Data Reveals

## Microsoft-backed Brazilian REDD scheme shows 39.9% forest loss since protection began — worse than surrounding areas left without safeguards

A carbon credit project deep in the Brazilian Amazon, registered to protect over 27,000 hectares of rainforest and backed by Microsoft, has lost nearly 40% of its forest cover since protection supposedly began — a rate of destruction that exceeds what happened in neighbouring areas that received no protection at all. That is the central, damning finding from satellite data analyzed by CarbonVerifier of the Cikel Brazilian Amazon REDD APD project, registered with Verra as VCS981.

The numbers are stark. Of the 20,970 hectares of forest that existed within the project boundary in 2000, some 8,367 hectares have been lost since the project's official start date of April 2009 — representing 39.9% of the original forest cover. These figures are computed within a rectangular bounding-box approximation centred on the project centroid; the exact registered polygon was not available for analysis, and headline numbers should be read with that caveat in mind.

**The satellite timeline tells a story of escalating failure.**

In the years immediately before the project launched, deforestation was already a serious concern. The area lost 505.7 hectares in 2004, 730.7 hectares in 2005, and 351.9 hectares in 2006 — totalling 2,077 hectares stripped before protection was ever claimed. But what followed project registration has been far worse.

The early post-2009 years showed relatively modest losses: 147 hectares in 2009, then a spike to 611 hectares in 2010 before a brief lull. The data then turns alarming. In 2014, 1,197 hectares vanished. In 2015, another 1,054.4 hectares. Then came 2016 — a single year in which 3,072.5 hectares of supposedly protected Amazon were destroyed, according to satellite data analyzed by CarbonVerifier. That figure alone exceeds the entire pre-project period loss, and represents a catastrophic breakdown of whatever safeguards were supposed to be in place.

The NDVI vegetation data from Sentinel-2 imagery reinforces this trajectory. The proportion of the project area classified as deforested stood at 16% in 2018. By 2023 it had risen to 13.7%, and by 2024 — the most recent scene captured on 3 September with zero cloud cover — it had climbed to 18.5%, with mean vegetation greenness falling to its lowest recorded value of 0.617. The forest cover fraction has declined from 74.7% in 2018 to just 69.6% in 2024.

**The additionality comparison may be the most troubling element of all.**

The entire premise of a REDD carbon credit is additionality: the forest is protected beyond what would have occurred anyway. To test this, satellite data analyzed by CarbonVerifier compared the project's post-start loss rate against five nearby unprotected control areas at the same latitude. The mean loss rate across those control areas was 22.04%. The project's loss rate was 39.9%. The difference — negative 17.86 percentage points — means the supposedly protected project lost forest at a rate nearly 18 points higher than unprotected jungle left to fend for itself.

Two of the five control areas lost forest at rates exceeding the project — 41.55% and 30.89% respectively — but three others lost only 14.2%, 11.31%, and 12.26% of their 2000 forest cover. The average across all five unprotected sites is, by any measure, considerably better than the outcome inside VCS981's boundaries.

**For the voluntary carbon market, the implications are serious.**

Microsoft, the only disclosed buyer of credits from this project, has used such purchases as part of its stated climate commitments. The satellite evidence raises fundamental questions about whether those credits represent real, measurable, additional carbon protection — the three pillars on which the credibility of the entire voluntary carbon market rests. If a registered, monitored Verra project can lose 8,367 hectares of Amazon forest after protection begins, and perform worse than areas with no protection whatsoever, it suggests that verification processes are failing to catch what orbital sensors can see in plain sight.
